heuristics.scenarioLabel: Early positions need full three-card quality; late unopened spots can lean more on the up card
heuristics.appliesWhen: Third-street stud-high starting hands before the bring-in is heavily contested
heuristics.adjustWhen: In early positions all three cards matter and the opening bar should be tighter
heuristics.rationale: The source makes position the first filter for whether a stud-high starter is playable or merely cosmetic.

heuristics.appliesWhen: Your first three cards are three-of-a-kind on third street
heuristics.rationale: The source calls rolled-up the best starting hand in Stud and explicitly recommends piling money in.

heuristics.scenarioLabel: Good stud draw starters stay playable only while their outs are live
heuristics.appliesWhen: High pair, concealed pair, three-flush, or three-straight style starter
heuristics.adjustWhen: Downgrade or fold flush and straight draws when multiple relevant outs are already exposed
heuristics.rationale: The source explicitly ties these starters to how many suit or straight outs are visible in other players' up cards.

heuristics.scenarioLabel: Three broadway-style high cards are playable unopened but fold to heavy third-street strength
heuristics.appliesWhen: Three high cards such as A-K down with J up in an unopened pot
heuristics.adjustWhen: Fold when facing both a completion and a raise
heuristics.rationale: The source gives an explicit three-high-card example with opposite advice depending on prior third-street aggression.

heuristics.scenarioLabel: Continue a third-street representation bluff mainly in heads-up pots when villain bricks fourth street
heuristics.appliesWhen: You completed or raised on third street to represent strength
heuristics.adjustWhen: Give up more often once two or more players continue unless your own hand improves considerably
heuristics.rationale: The source explicitly anchors bluff continuation to player count and how well the new up card connects with the opponent's visible structure.

heuristics.scenarioLabel: As the heads-up aggressor, pressure visible brick cards
heuristics.appliesWhen: Heads-up stud pot after you showed aggression earlier in the hand
heuristics.adjustWhen: Do not auto-fire when the new up card obviously strengthens their visible pair, draw, or made-hand line
heuristics.rationale: The source closes by stating that heads-up aggressors can often win simply by betting when the opponent's new up card appears to miss.
